behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: This is a pure backoff policy rule (failure count -> delay, capped by config) and can fail inside one local function. No module handoff, real I/O, or model output is required. Higher levels are disqualified by the behavior text focusing only on delay math.
behaviors.test_description: Unit-test the backoff calculator with consecutive failure counts and a max cap; assert exponential growth (e.g., 100, 200, 400...) and cap enforcement.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: The likely failure boundary is response-classification + retry scheduler/backoff wiring (module handoff), not just one formula. Real network is not required; a stubbed transport/response object is enough. Not Agentic/Workflow because no AI decisions are involved.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with a fake HTTP transport returning a 429 (and retry metadata if applicable), wired to retry logic + backoff policy; assert it is classified as rate-limit and the computed next wait matches policy/header precedence.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: This depends on orchestration across timer/wait handling, request replay, and result propagation. A unit test of one function could miss wiring bugs. System is not required because in-process fake clock + stub transport can reproduce this failure mode.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with fake timers and stub transport: first response is rate-limited, second succeeds; advance time to scheduled retry, assert original request payload is resent and final returned value is the successful response.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: The failure is at the boundary between retry-attempt tracking and public error mapping/type surface. Pure unit logic may miss incorrect plumbing in the `call()` flow. No real OS/network/LLM dependency is needed.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test where stub transport always returns rate-limit; run through configured max retries and assert rejection is `QuotaExhaustedError` with expected typed fields, not raw/generic upstream error.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: This is explicitly multi-module local orchestration (retry engine + backoff + quota tracker) and termination-state correctness. It does not require real OS/network subprocess behavior; deterministic in-process fakes can expose infinite-loop/termination bugs. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with fake timers and always-rate-limited stub responses; execute entire chain and assert bounded number of attempts, eventual `QuotaExhaustedError`, and no further scheduled retries after terminal state.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: The failure appears when concurrent callers interact through shared in-process state (module interaction/concurrency), not within one isolated function. Behavior text says 'same process', which disqualifies System-level real distributed infra needs.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test launching multiple concurrent `call()` invocations against a shared client instance and controlled stub transport; assert quota decrements globally across callers and rate-limit behavior reflects one shared bucket.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: This is queueing/scheduler behavior across async submitter, waiter queue, and dispatcher modules. Unit tests on queue internals alone can miss integration ordering regressions. No real OS/network/LLM requirement is stated.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with controlled clock and stub transport: enqueue multiple blocked requests, release capacity gradually, and assert dispatch/resolve order is FIFO by original submission sequence.
